comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on toward midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a relevant quote: 
 
"Success and integrity are not matters of opportunity, but evolve in character. The noblest, wisest, and most virtuous human beings are not those who have had the greatest opportunities. Those whose difficulties are numerous frequently develop the best philosophies of life; those whose problems are petty bewail their fate the loudest." 
 
That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book, one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism, and a reminder that over-the-top political passions are nothing new. (The image to the left, on the other hand, is one of Augustus Knapp's splendid illustrations for Hall's The Secret Teachings of All Ages.)     

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 

One other thing. Please do not ask for medical or health care advice here. Practicing medicine without a license is illegal in the US, where I reside; if the medical industry decides to get nasty, even offering advice in a forum like this one can be considered practicing medicine without a license, and people do jail time for that. That's the legal reality these days, and until the corporate kleptocracy loses its deathgrip on health care, I have to take that into consideration. Thank you for abiding by this!

[IMAGE:1]
With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- see you next week!***

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

comments.title: Magic for people with heart issues
comments.content: Hello John. I remember you mentioning that occultism isn’t a good idea for people with cardiovascular issues. Do you happen to know what kinds of magical things can such a person safely do? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Magic for people with heart issues
replies.content: The only occult practices that aren't not safe for people with heart conditions are the more intense ends of ceremonial magic and clairvoyance, because both of these can put stress on the heart. Everything else -- meditation, divination, the less demanding forms of ritual, natural magic, and the rest -- is fine for people with heart conditions, and according to the studies I've seen, meditation is actively good for many heart conditions (though you should of course check with your health care provider).

replies.content: So, Middle Pillar yay, evocation nay?

replies.title: Re: Magic for people with heart issues

replies.title: Re: Working With Spirits As Jungian Avatars
replies.content: Okay, a lot of people think that if they approach spirits as archetypes they won't have to deal with the spirits as persons -- they can remain comfortably in what Martin Buber called an "I-it" relationship, a relationship of manipulating something that's passive and unconscious, and not have to take the risks of an "I-you" relationship, of meeting another being on an equal level. All you have to do is read Jung and you'll find out that it doesn't work that way. Archetypes express themselves in our consciousness as persons -- either we project them on human beings, or we experience them directly as nonhuman beings, but either way, you're face to face with someone who's looking back at you and may or may not behave the way you want them to behave...

From what I've seen so far, it seems common for mages to end up having a non-linear view of time. I was recently listening to a podcast with Lon Milo duquette where he said that he tended towards a non-linear view of reincarnation, where your next incarnation could be in the past relative to your present life, and that it would be possible to actually shake hands and have dinner with one of your past or future incarnation without knowing it ! I also heard about chaos magiciens using the quantum physics concept of retrocausality in which future events influence present circumstances. Otherwise, I also heard from mages having the view that every moment past and future would be happening simultaneously, like pannels in a comic strip...I'm wondering, what is your view of time in a nutshell ?

replies.title: Re: time
replies.content: I start from the recognition that human beings aren't very bright and there are many things in the cosmos we can't understand. Thus I don't worry about having a single view of time. Retrocausality is a common experience for operative mages -- it's routine for the successful results of a spell to depend on events that happened before the spell was cast, or even thought of -- and eternity, the state in which all time is simultaneously present, is a common experience for mystics; thus it's clear that both of those are part of the whole phenomenon of time. Beyond that, I don't worry about it much.

replies.title: Re: time
replies.content: Not OP, but going to piggyback: has the occult community experimented with getting retrocausal results and then deliberately not following through on the working that they were intending to do? If so, what resulted?

replies.title: Re: time
replies.content: I don't know of anyone who's done that. It would seem counterproductive, though -- if retrocausality works, the fact that you didn't do the working would also affect the result in the past, of course.

comments.title: Influence of Judaic mysticism
comments.content:
With devouring so much Judaic mysticism over the past few days, I recall how often I've seen reference to the Kaballah, sometimes as the Qaballah, to Zohar, the Tree of Life, which seems to be commonly used, along with a lot of terminology with is Hebrew in origin.. it has made me ponder how much influence have Judaic ideas given to the lens from which modern Western occult and esotericism have derived their understanding? Is it to such an extent that spending a lot of time understanding Judaic mysticism is useful first step in developing more understanding of the occult mysteries?

Thank you as always for your insights and shared knowledge,
Prizm

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: A great deal of classic Western occultism comes through Judaism. Most of that wasn't Jewish in origin -- as Gershom Scholem, the premier historian of Kabbalistic thought, points out in The Origins of the Kabbalah, the Kabbalah was simply the Jewish version of the broad classical occult tradition that became Gnosticism in Christianity and theurgy in Hellenism. The Gnostics and the Pagan theurgists were exterminated by Christian persecution, however, while the Jews in some parts of the Mediterranean world managed to hang on to their inner teachings; that's why the occultists of the Renaissance turned to Jewish sources to fill in the gaps in what they got from the ancient world. As to whether studying Jewish mysticism is a good idea for your occult studies, that really varies from person to person; some swear by it, some swear at it.

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content:
It sounds like the history of the occult follows similar trajectories with the history of anything! Which is to say, I'm not surprised that it happened that a piece of something survived and from that we filled in some gaps. Thanks for the reference to The Origins of the Kabbalah. There are so many things to read but at the moment this does seem to be resonating with me. Since your book The Secret of the Temple happened to be on sale at this coincidental time I was more than happy to get a copy of that as well. I think I will find it useful, along with finding more things to read. My wife on the other will be upset we've got another book! hah

Prizm

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: Exactly -- the history of the occult is part of the broader history of ideas and of culture, and follows the usual patterns.

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: Is there a reason why Judaism seems to do such a good job of preserving things?

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: Old mundane astrology teachings have it that the Jewish faith is ruled by the planet Saturn, and Saturnine energies are nothing if not retentive!

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: Interestingly, Islamic scholars preserved classical greek philosophy, by translating into arabic, inadvertently protecting it from the Christian scythe that cut down so much else. Talmudism seems to have done us a similar good turn!

replies.title: Re: Influence of Judaic mysticism
replies.content: So did the Arabs -- a fantastic amount of what Europe knew about classical culture got to them via translations from the Arabic.

comments.title: playing cards
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I like to shuffle playing cards, and I'm wondering what magic I can do with playing cards. Is there anything you'd recommend for someone who hasn't got magical experience?

Thanks,
A

replies.title: Re: playing cards
replies.content: People do tarot readings with regular playing cards. They historically evolved from the tarot deck, so they have the same meaning as (a subset of) the tarot.

replies.title: Re: playing cards
replies.content: Playing cards have been used for divination for a very long time -- before Tarot cards, in fact. There are many books on the subject, and at least one organization, the International Association of Cardology, which works with the system of playing card-based teachings developed in the 19th century by American occultist Olney H. Richmond and his Order of the Magi. Do some searching on your favorite online bookstore or search engine and you'll find plenty to work with.

replies.title: Re: playing cards
replies.content:
Hi,
you are in the realm of what is broadly called Cartomancy.
Welcome!
Tarot is a well known form, but there is also Lenormand (a lot more literal usually and easier to start with as there are half the cards). Use a keyword search on both of those terms to find a form you enjoy.
In terms of mapping playing cards to Tarot - you're effectively using the minor arcana - the 'trumps' have dropped off. A quick search will give you the correspondences, just don't try asking anything too deep or esoteric. Without the major arcana (the trump cards) it may read like only have an answer.
Best wishes on your journey.

comments.title: Great videos of rituals?
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Great videos of rituals?
replies.content:
You won't find anything like that. Remember that Reconstructionism is, ahem, reconstructed, and it's going to be homespun and rather awkward for the next century or two. Doubtless when early Christians fumbled their way through the very first versions of their ceremonies, they looked equally homespun and awkward, compared to the timeless elegance of the ceremonies going on at the local temple of Jupiter. "Nothing new was ever done, except awkwardly."

As far as "accurate," we have next to no knowledge of what ancient Druid ceremonies entailed. Nobody alive today knows what an accurate ancient Druid ceremony would be like!

comments.title: Guardian
comments.content:
Hi JMG, long story followed by a few questions.

For the first time (and probably last for a long while) I had a go at gambling and put some money down on Trump to win. I usually have a sense about elections, but this time it was all fog. Still, my reason said it was the likely outcome so I gave it a go. On election night he was pulling ahead and I went to bed fairly confident. I slept lightly and woke up at 3am (I'm in the UK) feeling completely awake, and as I was in the bathroom I heard a voice, very clear and loud in my head, that said: "You should cash out now before they take it from him." At another time in my life I might have dismissed it as nerves, but I went and checked and lo and behold, that was right at the point when his odds were the shortest. I sat for a few minutes as they started to get longer again, cashed out and doubled my money.

A few times in my life when I felt like I was on the ropes I tried reaching out for help, and each time felt like there was a kind of comforting presence, out there, that was pulling for me. I don't have any magical training, but my guess would be that it was a kind of guardian spirit? But I think this is the first time that it ever actually spoke to me!

My questions are these:

1) Do you think I'm correct in my assumption about what contacted me?

2) If so, what would be the best way to go about communicating? I've tried asking questions in my head, and it seems to answer, but...

3) How can I tell that apart from whatever my subconscious might conjure up?

Much obliged.

comments.title: Vienna
comments.content:
There was a terrorist attack last Monday night in Vienna and I was very startled to find out that my tram must have left just a few minutes before the shooting occurred there. Not only that, but I felt my guardian angel's influence.

I'd been sitting in a cafe at Schwedenplatz and had planned to stay there till it closed at 8, which would have put me at the tram stop right when it happened. Instead I had this feeling of "You're bored, tired, and would rather be home." I'm not the type to get bored, and I was hopped up on caffeine, so I fought the repeated feeling for about half an hour till 7:40 when I gave in and left.

Then I went to the grocery nearby, and again had unusual feelings. I'm typically bedazzled in the grocery and spend a lot of time there, but this time I felt a strange contentment, "Jogurt's on sale? Okay grab some. Now you're completely satisfied and would rather be home." That put me waiting at the tram stop sometime close to the hour, and I got home shortly after 8.

This was quite a shock to me. I didn't sleep the first night. Then there was the emotional reaction of feeling my HGA's intervention and protection so strongly. I do the MM journaling exercise to talk with my Higher Self (or maybe it's my HGA or subconscious, I don't know), but this time I didn't get any kind of response, even though I'm not distraught anymore.

This culminates at a time when I'd been evaluating my priorities because I accomplished a life goal last month: I'd always wanted to write a novel series, and I've finished the last book. I was also surprised to realize I was content about it, even though the books have only achieved mild success. Instead of putting that as primacy and churning out a ton of books and maybe accruing success, I felt like I wanted to slow my roll and put spirituality first. A few weeks ago on a whim I wrote "Forty Hours of Spiritual Study Per Week" on a note card on my desk and was surprised to find myself utterly thrilled about recording the hours to see if I could get there.

All that is to say, I'm wondering whether you have any advice for me, since I didn't hear anything from my Higher Self in the journaling exercise. Every moment holds weight, and I feel like I've been handed something I may not deserve, and I don't want to fumble it. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Vienna
replies.content: I've had things like that happen. At a certain point in one's spiritual development, it becomes possible to tune into that kind of emergency warning. Relax, thank your HGA, and go on with your spiritual study!

replies.title: Re: CGD Sickle Consecration Terminology
replies.content: "Phantoms" in that ritual indicates spirits of a low grade; it's a traditional term in some occult teachings. As for the broader issue, it's quite simple. comments.title: Omens
comments.content:
I have not done any explicit divinations regarding any outcomes of the US election, but on the night of the election, the end of a long day out with my family, my final financial transaction came to ¥1776— an amount I can say with some confidence I have never been charged for anything before.

Once we got home, just as I was attempting to explain the sugnificance of that number to my non-American wife, our 3-year-old decided she wanted to give us a “letter” she’d “written” (scribbled). First she tried to give it to me; then she wanted to give it to mommy; then, somewhat perplexed, she decided to cut the letter in half and give us each half... and I have never seen a three year old cut such a geometrically perfect zigzag! We each duly received our half of a letter full of a child’s love.

After that I went to look at the sky for a bit (one of that week’s will exercises). A cloudy night— wait, over there, the sky is clear— that star is so bright it must be a planet. Intensely bright, in fact. Calling me to it. Red? Is that Mars? I went back inside and went online. Indeed, Mars was literally the only planet above the horizon at that time in my location.

So, 1776 — split — Mars. These were my omens, and I share them here, for whatever they’re worth. (Four years ago, the omens I got were an intensely windy day filled with rain, sun, and several rainbow sightings, as well as my coworker having her baby delivered more or less precisely as the results came in.)

I shared these omens with a close (Trump loathing) friend, and told her that I read the 1776 omen as meaning that this is a moment of reboot, a turning of the great wheel for our country; she told me that it sounds in line with what she’d been hearing recently that the astrological conditions in America right now are extremely similar to how they were at the time of the Revolutionary War.

JMG, do you know anything about this and do you concur at all? Poking about on the subject a bit online, it seems that 2008-2024 is America’s Pluto Return, going by the Sibley chart. Which would actually put the Obama administration at the start of it...

I am guessing, based on what you’ve written about Pluto before, that you may not find this relationship compelling... but then again, maybe you do. Do you have any comment? And, have you looked at the American Revolutionary War’s astrological weather using your own preferred tools of mundane astrology? Do you see any kind of connection with today at all?

A second question, if I may.

How do I work the kind of deep revisions being asked of me--changes to core beliefs and fundamental values--without feeling (or, more likely, in spite of feeling) like my soul's being ripped out of me? For example, I'm told, reminded, instructed that I'm not here to do great things, but to develop my soul. And the Carrot of Truth is all about non-comparison. But without comparison, we have no benchmark. Without ideals, however unattainable, we have nothing against which to measure our achievement.

Just a an example from recent events, I've always seen political leadership in a democratic society as a very high calling. Likewise, I've always seen the ideal citizen as one who is knowledgeable in the topics of the times and able to converse intelligently with others on key issues facing the community. Serving on city council ought to have made me fulfilled, being an active decisionmaker and helping to guide my community; instead, it slowly drained me and after three years I was done. This is not how it should be and yet it is. I'm being asked detach from politics, but this is the opposite of what I've seen as valuable. I'm being directed inward, told that my soul's development is vastly more important than the course of human civilization or the fate of my nation, but I have great difficulty seeing how that can be. I've always held knowledge as the highest good, yet I'm being told I need to dilute that good with other aspects of self in order to achieve balance. I conceptually understand the strength of flow and adaptability, but in the application, it comes off as being passive and a doormat. I still desire to bend experience to my will, but I know that I lack the power to do so. This causes great emotional difficulty, which just highlights the issues with emotions generally. comments.title: On giving advice
comments.content:
This morning a friend reached out to me regarding starting a magical practice, particularly for resources for starting up a practice of Chaos magic. I haven't responded yet, but the advice I'm thinking of giving is as follows:

1: Have a clear idea of what you want out of the magical practice. Some people want to change their lives, and that can mean they want to change themselves, some people want to heal, some people want to develop awareness of the less mundane world hidden from us by our society's collective madness of rational materialism- and all of these are worthy goals. But different magic systems do different things, and it's worthwhile to meditate upon each path.

2. Speaking of which, meditate! No matter what path you choose, it's helpful(side note: here is where I was going to recommend your posts on discursive meditation as a place to start)

3. No matter what you decide to practice, make a banishing ritual (hopefully daily) part of that regular practice. Even better, banish before you meditate.

Do you think this is good advice? Any pointers or edits are, as always, greatly appreciated.

Also, I don't know much about good starting resources for Chaos magic- would you happen to have any recommendations?

replies.content: That sounds like very good advice. The one thing I'd add is a recommendation to take up some system of divination and practice it daily. It's important to be able to listen to the magical universe, as well as speaking to it! As for resources for Chaos magic, I'm out of the loop there; it's not my style at all, and so the only thing on the subject I've ever read was Peter Carroll's Liber Null and Psychonaut.
